questions.question: Where are Docker volumes stored by default on a Linux host?
questions.description: Volumes are managed by Docker and live in the host filesystem at /var/lib/docker/volumes. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Which mount type is best for sharing source code from your host into a development container?
questions.description: Bind mounts map a specific host path to the container, making them ideal for dev workflows. Check: [Bind Mounts](/storage/bind-mounts)

questions.question: What happens to a Named Volume when the container using it is deleted?

options.text: The volume persists on the host
The volume is moved to the registry
The volume is cleared of all data

questions.description: Volumes have a lifecycle independent of containers. They persist until explicitly removed. Check: [When to Use Which?](/storage/when-to-use)

questions.question: Which command removes all volumes NOT currently used by at least one container?
questions.description: The prune command is the standard way to cleanup unused resources. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: tmpfs mounts are stored in:
questions.description: tmpfs is a memory-only mount, making it fast but non-persistent. Check: [tmpfs Mounts](/storage/tmpfs-mounts)

questions.question: True or False: A bind mount can map a single file, not just a whole directory.
questions.description: You can bind mount individual files like /etc/resolv.conf. Check: [Bind Mounts](/storage/bind-mounts)

questions.question: Which flag is recommended for creating volumes in production for better clarity?
questions.description: --mount is more verbose but prevents common mistakes. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What happens if you bind mount a host directory containing files over a container directory that also has files?

options.text: The host files obscure (hide) the container files
The container files overwrite the host files

questions.description: The mount 'covers' the existing content of the container directory. Check: [Bind Mounts](/storage/bind-mounts)

questions.question: Where does Docker Desktop for Windows store volumes by default?

options.text: Inside a hidden WSL2 distribution or Hyper-V VM

questions.description: Docker Desktop uses a utility VM or WSL2 backend. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: How do you identify which containers are using a specific volume?
questions.description: The container's inspection data lists active mounts. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: You want a container to read configuration from a file on the host but ensure it cannot modify it. Which option do you use?
questions.description: Appending :ro to a mount makes it read-only. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Anonymous volumes are primarily created when:

options.text: You forget to name a volume in 'docker run -v'
You use 'docker volume create' without a name

questions.description: Anonymous volumes have random hex names and are usually ephemeral. Check: [When to Use Which?](/storage/when-to-use)

questions.question: To force the removal of an anonymous volume when its container is deleted, use:
questions.description: The -v flag on 'docker rm' cleans up associated anonymous volumes. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Which command lists all volumes available to the local Docker engine?
questions.description: Standard management command. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: You need to share a database across two containers on the same host. What is the most reliable mount type?
questions.description: Named volumes are the production standard for container data sharing. Check: [When to Use Which?](/storage/when-to-use)

questions.question: What is a 'Volume Driver'?

options.text: A plugin that allows Docker to use external storage (S3, NFS, etc.)
A tool to compress volumes on disk

questions.description: Volume drivers extend Docker storage capabilities beyond the local host. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Can you bind mount a file that doesn't exist yet?

options.text: Yes, Docker will create an empty file

questions.description: For files, the source MUST exist or Docker will throw an error (or create a directory instead if using -v). Check: [Bind Mounts](/storage/bind-mounts)

questions.question: You are debugging a container and want to see exactly where its volume is mapped on the host. Which command helps?
questions.description: Inspect shows the 'Mountpoint' on the host. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What happens if you try to remove a volume that is still in use by a stopped container?

options.text: The volume is deleted and container is corrupted
The volume is marked for deletion after next start

questions.description: Volumes cannot be removed while referenced by any container. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Which mount type is Linux-only?
questions.description: tmpfs relies on Linux kernel memory management features. Check: [tmpfs Mounts](/storage/tmpfs-mounts)

questions.question: Which storage driver is the modern standard for Linux?
questions.description: overlay2 is the preferred union filesystem driver. Check: [overlay2](/storage/drivers/overlay2)

questions.question: What is 'Copy-on-Write' (CoW)?

options.text: Sharing a single set of files until one container needs to modify them
Writing files to multiple volumes at once
A version control system for images

questions.description: CoW maximizes disk efficiency by sharing identical layers. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: Which layer is the only writable layer in a running container?
questions.description: Image layers are read-only; only the top container layer accepts changes. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: If you change the storage driver on a live system, what happens to existing images?

options.text: They become inaccessible to the new driver

questions.description: Changing drivers is a breaking change for existing data. Check: [Select a Storage Driver](/storage/drivers/select)

questions.question: Which driver uses 'Snapshotters' instead of 'Graph Drivers'?
questions.description: Modern containerd integration uses the snapshotter architecture. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Where is the storage driver configured?
questions.description: Configurations go in /etc/docker/daemon.json. Check: [overlay2](/storage/drivers/overlay2)

questions.question: How many layers can 'overlay2' stack max?
questions.description: Overlay2 has a hard limit of 128 layers. Check: [overlay2](/storage/drivers/overlay2)

questions.question: Which driver is best for system integrity and block-level snapshots?
questions.description: ZFS provides advanced filesystem features at the expense of high RAM. Check: [ZFS](/storage/drivers/zfs)

questions.question: What is the 'lowerdir' in OverlayFS?

options.text: The stack of read-only image layers

questions.description: The lowerdir contains the static image content. Check: [overlay2](/storage/drivers/overlay2)

questions.question: Which driver is the slowest and most disk-heavy due to lack of CoW?
questions.description: VFS makes full 'deep copies' for every layer. Check: [VFS](/storage/drivers/vfs)

questions.question: You are on a system where the kernel does not support union filesystems. What will Docker likely fall back to?
questions.description: VFS is the universal fallback for any Linux kernel. Check: [VFS](/storage/drivers/vfs)

questions.question: What specifically does XFS need enabled to support overlay2?
questions.description: OverlayFS requires d_type support from the underlying filesystem. Check: [overlay2](/storage/drivers/overlay2)

questions.question: Why does ZFS require high RAM (often 1GB+ purely for cache)?

options.text: It loads all images into RAM

questions.description: ZFS's ARC is a sophisticated caching layer that consumes significant memory. Check: [ZFS](/storage/drivers/zfs)

questions.question: Which command shows your active storage driver?
questions.description: Check the 'Storage Driver' line in docker info. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: What happens if a container modifies a 10GB file in the read-only layer for the first time?

options.text: Only the block changes are saved
The entire 10GB file is copied to the writable layer first
The file is edited in place (dangerous)

questions.description: This is the 'Copy-Up' overhead; it copies the target file in its entirety. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: You want to maximize image building speed by reusing cached layers. Which driver architecture facilitates this best?
questions.description: Layering is the core of Docker cache efficiency. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: True or False: Storage drivers are responsible for managing Volumes.
questions.description: Storage drivers manage internal image/container layers. Volumes are separate. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: Which driver is best for 'high-density' servers where many containers use the same base image?
questions.description: Overlay2 allows for shared page cache in memory, saving huge amounts of RAM. Check: [overlay2](/storage/drivers/overlay2)

questions.question: The 'AUFS' driver is best known as:

options.text: The original union filesystem for early Docker

questions.description: AUFS was the pioneer but has been replaced by overlay2. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: You find that your container's disk space is filling up despite mounting all large data to a volume. Why?

options.text: Logs or temp files are being written to the container layer

questions.description: Anything not in a volume goes to the driver-managed writable layer. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview)

questions.question: What does 'Mount Propagation' control?

options.text: How mounts inside the container interact with host mounts

questions.description: Propagation defines if sub-mounts are shared between Host and Container. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive#senior-topic-mount-propagation)

questions.question: Which propagation setting is the default?
questions.description: Private means mounts in one namespace aren't visible in others. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive#senior-topic-mount-propagation)

questions.question: You have a volume named 'db_data'. What is the safest way to back it up while the DB is semi-active?

options.text: Use a worker container with --volumes-from and run 'tar'
Use 'docker cp' on every file individually

questions.description: The worker container pattern is the official recommendation for volume backup. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What is the 'shared' propagation mode used for?

options.text: Allowing a container mount to be seen by the host's other mounts

questions.description: Shared mode reflects changes in both directions (host and container). Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive#senior-topic-mount-propagation)

questions.question: How do you specify a volume as read-only in 'docker run'?
questions.description: The :ro suffix is the standard way to restrict writes. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What happens if you use ':z' (lowercase) in a mount on an SELinux system?

options.text: It relabels the folder as 'private' for only this container
It disables SELinux for that folder

questions.description: Lowercase z marks the mount as private to one container. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What happens if you use ':Z' (uppercase) in a mount on an SELinux system?

options.text: It relabels the folder as 'shared' among multiple containers

questions.description: Uppercase Z allows multiple containers to access the same volume under SELinux. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What was the 'Data-only container' pattern?

options.text: A way to run containers without images
An old (obsolete) way to manage volumes before Docker volumes were first-class

questions.description: Legacy Docker required creating actual containers just to hold data volumes. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Why would you use 'tmpfs' instead of a Volume for a web server password?

options.text: To hide it from the host OS
To ensure it never touches the physical disk and disappears on reboot
To make it faster to access

questions.description: Security and non-persistence are the primary drivers for tmpfs. Check: [tmpfs Mounts](/storage/tmpfs-mounts)

questions.question: You need to mount a volume to a Swarm Service. Which flag is REQUIRED?
questions.description: Swarm services ONLY support the verbose --mount syntax. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Can you share a local host volume across multiple machines in a Swarm cluster natively?

options.text: No, you need a shared backend like NFS or a Cloud plugin

questions.description: Standard volumes are local to the machine they were created on. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Which management command helps you see the labels attached to a volume?
questions.description: Inspect returns full JSON metadata including labels. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: You want to clear all stopped containers AND their associated anonymous volumes in one command.
questions.description: The --volumes flag extends the system prune to include volume cleanup. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What is 'Unbindable' propagation mode?

options.text: Prevents any sub-mounts from being used
Mark a mount as private AND prevent it from being shared even if parent is shared

questions.description: Unbindable is the most restrictive propagation setting. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive#senior-topic-mount-propagation)

questions.question: TRUE or FALSE: Docker volumes can be used to mount an NFS share directly.
questions.description: Using volume-opt, you can map NFS directly at the Docker level. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: An application is writing TBs of data. Should you use the Storage Driver layer or a Volume?
questions.description: Volumes bypass the CoW overhead and offer native filesystem performance. Check: [When to Use Which?](/storage/when-to-use)

questions.question: If you use '--mount type=bind,source=/a/b,target=/c', what happens if '/a/b' doesn't exist?

options.text: Docker creates it as a folder
Docker creates it as a file
Docker throws an error and fails to start

questions.description: This is the Missing Path Trap; --mount is explicit. Check: [Drivers Overview](/storage/drivers/overview#dca-exam-practice)

questions.question: Which command allows you to rename a volume?

options.text: There is no rename command (must copy and recreate)

questions.description: Volumes cannot be renamed once created. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: Can you limit the size of a volume on disk natively in Docker?

options.text: Only for certain drivers (e.g., tmpfs size, or XFS/Btrfs quotas)

questions.description: Quotas are usually dependent on the underlying driver and filesystem support. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What is the Difference between 'docker volume prune' and 'docker system prune --volumes'?

options.text: System prune cleans images/containers too; volume prune only affects volumes

questions.description: Pruning scope is the primary difference. Check: [Volumes](/storage/volumes-deep-dive)

questions.question: What is the main goal of the containerd image store integration?

options.text: To unify Docker with modern containerd snapshotter logic

questions.description: Unification with containerd enables modern features. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: What replaced 'Graph Drivers' in the containerd architecture?
questions.description: Snapshotters are the modern way to manage layered filesystems. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: How do you enable containerd store in Docker Desktop?

options.text: Via 'Use containerd for pulling and storing images' in Settings
It is always on by default since 2012

questions.description: It is a feature flag in the settings dashboard. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Does the containerd store support Windows containers?
questions.description: Containerd architecture is designed to be cross-platform. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: What is a 'Snapshot' in containerd terms?

options.text: An individual layer of an image (either read-only or active)
A full backup of the host

questions.description: Snapshots are the building blocks of containerd storage. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: True or False: The containerd store allows ARM and x86 images for the same tag to reside on the same host.
questions.description: Multi-platform image management is a key containerd feature. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: You want to run a WebAssembly (Wasm) container. Which storage backend facilitates this best?
questions.description: Wasm integration is tied to the modern containerd architecture. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Why is image push/pull faster in some containerd workflows?

options.text: It skips the redundant extraction step on some systems

questions.description: Integration reduces extraction overhead. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Where are containerd store feature toggles stored on Linux?
questions.description: The 'containerd-snapshotter' key is added to features in daemon.json. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: In containerd store, where are the local image artifacts actually saved?
questions.description: A dedicated directory next to the standard docker folders. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: What is 'Content Addressable Storage'?

options.text: Identifying data by its location on disk
Identifying data by its cryptographic hash (SHA256)
Storing data only in the cloud

questions.description: This ensures data integrity and efficient deduplication. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Can you use older Graph Drivers like Overlay2 simultaneously with the containerd store?

options.text: No, you must choose one architecture

questions.description: Enabling containerd snapshotters replaces the legacy graph driver path. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Which tool would you use to inspect the 'unfiltered' state of containerd snapshotters?
questions.description: ctr is the low-level maintenance tool for containerd. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: What is the 'native' snapshotter best used for?
questions.description: Native is the simple directory fallback in the containerd world. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Does the containerd store significantly increase disk usage?

options.text: Yes, because it stores both compressed and uncompressed blobs

questions.description: Currently, the dual-storage model uses more space than legacy Overlay2. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: True or False: The containerd store is required to run Wasm workloads in Docker.
questions.description: Wasm runtimes are integrated into the containerd backend. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: You see 'Snapshotter: overlay' in 'docker info'. What does this mean?

options.text: You have the containerd store enabled with the overlay snapshotter

questions.description: Snapshotter info only appears when the containerd store is active. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: The containerd image store is based on which industry standard?
questions.description: OCI compliance ensures interoperability. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: How does containerd handle layer sharing between different images?

options.text: Deduplication via SHA256 of the content blobs
It stores all images in one folder

questions.description: Content-addressability allows for extreme efficient sharing. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)

questions.question: Can you use official Docker Hub images with the containerd store?
questions.description: The change is internal; external image sources remain compatible. Check: [Containerd Image Store](/storage/containerd-store)
